questions.question: Explain argc and argv in command line arguments with an example.
keyPoints: argc stands for argument count - number of arguments passed including program name
argv stands for argument vector - array of character pointers storing arguments as strings
Example: ./myprog file.txt 10 → argc=3, argv[0]="./myprog", argv[1]="file.txt", argv[2]="10"

questions.question: Write a C program to add two numbers passed as command line arguments.
keyPoints: Include necessary header files (stdio.h, stdlib.h)
Define main with int main(int argc, char* argv[])
Validate argc to ensure sufficient arguments provided
Convert string arguments to integers using atoi()
Perform addition and print result
Return appropriate exit status

questions.question: What is the purpose of validating command line arguments? Give an example of validation code.
keyPoints: Purpose: Prevent undefined behavior, crashes, or wrong results when insufficient/wrong arguments provided
Check if argc >= minimum required + 1 (for program name)
Print usage message to stderr if validation fails
Return non-zero exit status to indicate error
Example: if(argc < 3) { fprintf(stderr, "Usage: %s num1 num2\n", argv[0]); return 1; }

questions.question: Differentiate between atoi() and strtol() functions.
keyPoints: atoi() - simple ASCII to integer, no error checking, returns 0 on failure
strtol() - provides error handling via errno, can detect partial conversions
strtol() can handle different number bases (base 10, 16, etc.)
strtol() sets endptr to indicate where conversion stopped
Recommendation: Use strtol() for production code requiring robust error handling

questions.question: Explain the significance of argv[0] in C programs.
keyPoints: argv[0] always contains the program name (executable filename)
It is automatically set by the operating system
May include full path depending on how program was invoked
Even when no arguments are passed, argc is at least 1 due to argv[0]
Can be used to display program name in error messages or for self-documentation

questions.question: Write a C program to multiply two floating-point numbers received as command line arguments.
keyPoints: Use atof() to convert string arguments to double
Validate argc to ensure at least 3 (program name + 2 numbers)
Perform multiplication operation
Display result with appropriate precision using %.2f format
Handle edge cases like zero or negative numbers
Return 0 on success, 1 on error

questions.question: Write a complete C program that accepts a filename and an integer N as command line arguments, reads the file, and prints the Nth line. Include proper error handling.
keyPoints: Include headers: stdio.h, stdlib.h
Validate argc must be exactly 3 (program, filename, N)
Extract filename from argv[1] and convert argv[2] to integer N using atoi() or strtol()
Open file using fopen() in read mode
Check if file opened successfully, handle errors
Implement line reading loop using fgets() or fgetc()
Count lines and stop at Nth line
Print the Nth line or error message if file has fewer than N lines
Close file and return appropriate status

questions.question: Explain getopt() function and write a program that accepts options -h (help), -v (version), -f (filename) using getopt().
keyPoints: getopt() is used for parsing Unix-style command line options
Defined in unistd.h header file
Works with getopt_long() for long options support
Returns option character when found, -1 when no more options
Option string defines expected options (e.g., "hvf:" where : means requires argument)
optarg global variable holds argument for options requiring values
optopt stores unknown option character
Program structure: while((c = getopt(argc, argv, "hvf:")) != -1) { switch(c) ... }
Show example handling each case with appropriate actions

questions.question: Write a C program to implement a simple calculator that accepts operation and two operands from command line. Example: ./calc add 10 20 should output 30.
keyPoints: Accept 4 arguments: program name, operation, operand1, operand2
Validate argc == 4, else print usage and exit
Extract operation string from argv[1]
Convert operand strings to integers using appropriate conversion
Use strcmp() to compare operation string
Implement cases: add, subtract, multiply, divide
Handle division by zero error
Print result or appropriate error messages
Return 0 on success, 1 on error

questions.question: Develop a C program that accepts multiple filenames from command line and displays total count of characters, words, and lines in all files combined.
keyPoints: argc indicates number of files to process
Loop through each file from argv[1] to argv[argc-1]
For each file: open with fopen(), handle error if fails
Initialize counters for chars, words, lines to zero
Read file character by character or using fgets()
Increment char count for each character
Increment line count for each newline ('\n')
Word detection: increment when whitespace follows non-whitespace
Accumulate totals across all files
Print individual file stats and grand totals
Close each file properly
Handle case of no files provided
